bull_thesis:
onsemi is a vertically integrated silicon carbide (SiC) and power-analog semiconductor leader sitting on the winning side of two long-term growth trends at once: electric-vehicle electrification and AI data-center power. The first quarter of 2026 (reported May 4, 2026) is the point where both turned up together.

On the automotive side, revenue grew 5% year over year — the first annual increase after seven consecutive declining quarters — driven by SiC content gains rather than unit volume. Management cites roughly 55% SiC design share among new electric-vehicle models shown at the April 2026 Beijing Auto Show, with China auto revenue rising even as China passenger-vehicle unit sales fell 6%. onsemi is the named preferred 900-volt power solution at Geely and NIO.

On the AI data-center side, revenue grew more than 30% in a single quarter — nearly double the high-teens growth management had guided — and is on track to double year over year in 2026 off a 2025 base that itself doubled. onsemi is engaged with all the leading hyperscalers across the power distribution chain, with more than 30 active FlexPower programs and multiple accelerator-chip customers.

The financial model is structurally sound. Non-GAAP (non-generally-accepted-accounting-principles) gross margin (revenue minus direct production costs, as a percentage of revenue) expanded for a third straight quarter to 38.5%, even in a seasonally slow quarter at only 77% factory utilization. That expansion through a volume trough is evidence of a structural cost and product-mix improvement under the FabRight program, not simply a volume tailwind — and it points to enormous operating leverage once volume returns. The long-term gross-margin target is above 50%. Full-year 2025 free cash flow (operating cash minus capital spending) hit a record $1.4 billion at a 24% margin through the worst of the cycle, and management repurchased $346 million of stock in the first quarter of 2026 at an average price of $60.54 — against a stock price of roughly $122 today. That buyback at close to half the current price is the clearest insider-confidence signal in the file; the people with the most information bought the bottom.

Lead times stretched from 23 to 26 weeks, signaling firming demand. The thesis requires auto SiC content gains to outpace Chinese average-selling-price erosion, the AI data-center leg to keep compounding, and production volume to eventually recover enough to drive the 38.5%-to-50% gross-margin bridge.
bear_thesis:
The bargain entry that made onsemi compelling is gone. The stock is up 117% over the past 12 months, re-rated from a deeply discounted cash-flow name to a roughly $48 billion market cap at about 28 times forward 2026 estimated earnings per share of $3.09 and about 22 times 2027 estimated earnings of $4.36. Much of the AI spending optionality is now in the price, and at least one published note already describes ON as overbought ahead of a slower fundamental recovery.

Four specific concerns:

First, the AI data-center revenue line has no published dollar denominator. Management gives growth rates — 30% quarter over quarter, doubling year over year — but withholds the starting figure. Working backward, the implied annual run rate is only roughly $300 to $450 million against $6 billion in total revenue. The market is paying a premium for years of compounding that must still actually arrive.

Second, revenue was still down 1% sequentially in the first quarter of 2026. The inflection management describes is real on a year-over-year basis and in order signals, but it has not yet shown up in sequential dollars, and full-year 2025 revenue of $6.0 billion sits well below the roughly $8.3 billion 2023 peak.

Third, the GAAP (generally accepted accounting principles) picture is weak. First-quarter GAAP earnings per share were negative $0.08, GAAP operating margin was negative 3.5%, and full-year 2025 GAAP operating margin collapsed from 25.0% to 1.4% on restructuring and impairment charges. The clean story only exists in non-GAAP numbers.

Fourth, Chinese SiC is a vicious price war, with BYD-affiliated suppliers, StarPower, and other domestic entrants commoditizing the substrate. A 55% design share does not guarantee durable margin, and the 38.5%-to-50% gross-margin bridge depends on volume, product mix, and factory divestitures that the company does not fully control. Return on invested capital (ROIC, the return generated on all capital deployed in the business) sits at roughly 11 to 12%, still below the cost of capital — consistent with a narrow rather than a wide competitive moat (durable pricing power).

The signal to watch: a sequential revenue miss, an AI data-center growth deceleration, or Chinese SiC average-selling-price compression that stalls the margin bridge.
sop_bull:
Sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) fair value: $165 to $185 per share.

Core auto and industrial power-analog (roughly $5.5 to $5.7 billion of estimated 2026 revenue at about 22 to 24% non-GAAP operating margin): on 2027 estimated earnings power of roughly $4.40 per share and a 30 times multiple — justified by a re-accelerating SiC franchise and the credible gross-margin bridge toward 50% — this piece is worth about $132 per share.

AI data-center leg as a separate growth option: roughly $0.6 to $0.8 billion in 2026 estimated revenue, growing toward more than $1.5 billion by 2027 to 2028, valued at 6 to 8 times forward sales (in line with hyperscaler-infrastructure power suppliers) — about $5 to $7 billion of enterprise value, or roughly $12 to $16 per share.

Net debt is modest (slightly net-indebted balance sheet), so there is no cash premium. But the commitment to return 100% of free cash flow, plus accretive buybacks, supports an additional $20 to $35 per share of compounding tailwind on 2027 estimates.

Sum: roughly $132 core plus $14 AI data center plus $20 to $35 buyback and compounding tailwind, totaling approximately $165 to $185.
sop_bear:
Sum-of-the-parts trough-normalized fair value: $78 to $92 per share.

Core power-analog: mid-cycle earnings estimate of roughly $3.00 per share at a de-rated 22 times multiple (capped by commodity-adjacent dynamics and the Chinese SiC price war) — about $66 per share.

AI data-center leg: discounted to roughly $0.5 billion run rate at 5 times forward sales, or about $2.5 billion of enterprise value, which is roughly $5 to $6 per share — then haircut further for execution risk.

GAAP losses and the modest net-debt position warrant no net-cash premium. Total: roughly $72 to $80 plus modest free-cash-flow support, or $78 to $92. At the current $122 price, the stock sits 30 to 40% above the bear case — the gap that opened after the 117% run.
sop_breakdown:
The base case blends a recovering but not yet booming core business with a real but still-small AI data-center option, at a multiple that acknowledges the 117% run already banked.

Core auto and industrial power-analog: 2026 estimated earnings of roughly $3.05 per share at 28 times — a premium for SiC content growth and the credible 38.5%-to-50% gross-margin bridge, discounted from the bull case for Chinese average-selling-price risk — yields about $85 per share of intrinsic value.

AI data-center option: roughly $0.65 billion in 2026 estimated revenue growing, valued at about 6 times forward sales, implies approximately $4 billion of enterprise value and roughly $9 to $10 per share.

Margin-bridge and operating-leverage optionality (volume returning at 38.5% gross margin and 77% utilization): worth approximately $20 to $25 per share.

100%-of-free-cash-flow return commitment plus accretive buybacks add roughly $8 to $10 per share.

Modest net debt is a slight drag. Total: roughly $122 to $128, base case approximately $124 — essentially at the current $122 price.

A peer sanity check: at roughly 28 times forward earnings, ON is the cheapest of the profitable power-semiconductor cohort (STMicroelectronics at roughly 60 times, Infineon at roughly 33 times, Power Integrations at roughly 31 times), while carrying the best free-cash-flow margin (24%) and the strongest AI data-center growth narrative. The multiple is defensible, but it is no longer cheap on an absolute basis.
risk_reward:
Roughly symmetric to slightly unfavorable at $122.

Upside to base fair value: the base case of roughly $124 is essentially flat from here. The bull case of $165 to $185 implies 35% to 52% upside — but only if the AI data-center compounding and auto re-acceleration both deliver on an already-elevated bar.

Downside to bear case: the bear target of $78 to $92 implies 25% to 36% downside on a sequential revenue miss, an AI data-center deceleration, or a Chinese SiC average-selling-price war worsening.

The asymmetry that existed at the $60 buyback price has been spent. Risk-to-reward is roughly 1.2 to 1 at best on the bull scenario, and roughly 1 to 1 on the base case.
catalyst_status: SOFT_EXTENDED / partially-FIRED. The load-bearing catalysts — (a) AI-data-center doubling and (b) the auto-SiC YoY inflection — are NOT discrete dated events; they are quarterly-compounding trends, and the FIRST and biggest leg of the re-rate has already FIRED (stock +117% over 12 months; the Q1-2026 'inflection point' print on 2026-05-04 already ran). Forward proof points are recurring earnings prints (next: Q2 2026, early-Aug 2026, guided $1.535-1.635B with sequential GM expansion and H2>H1) — real but un-dated and continuation-dependent, not a binding single-binary. No hyperscaler award is contractually disclosed with a dollar value; the design-win claims (all hyperscalers, Treo/T1S, Geely/NIO 900V) are management-asserted, not third-party-verified. Net: the asymmetric, pre-catalyst window has closed; what remains is trend-continuation that must keep beating an already-elevated bar.
peer_comps_note:
The true peer group within the power-semiconductor and SiC layer, with live multiples verified June 22, 2026:

STMicroelectronics (STM): forward price-to-earnings of roughly 60 times, enterprise-value-to-EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, divided into enterprise value) of roughly 29 times — distressed trough earnings inflate the multiple; the most direct SiC and automotive competitor.

Infineon (IFX): forward price-to-earnings of roughly 33 times, enterprise-value-to-EBITDA of roughly 25 times — the broadest power-semiconductor peer, with exposure to electric vehicles, industrial, and AI data-center.

Power Integrations (POWI): forward price-to-earnings of roughly 31 times — high-voltage and AI data-center power, smaller and more focused.

Wolfspeed (WOLF): post-bankruptcy, with negative earnings and EBITDA — the cautionary tale for a pure-play SiC supplier.

On the metrics that matter most, onsemi screens best in class: the lowest forward price-to-earnings among the profitable group (roughly 28 times), the highest free-cash-flow margin (24%, a record), a gross margin of 38.5% and rising, arguably the strongest near-term AI data-center growth (doubling on doubling), and the auto-SiC content lead (roughly 55% of Beijing design wins) that the pure-play power names lack.

On valuation, onsemi ranks cheapest in the cohort. On 2026 and 2027 estimated earnings growth (up roughly 31% and 41%), it ranks near the top. On free cash flow and gross margin, it ranks first. On return on invested capital (roughly 11 to 12%), it ranks in the middle, weighed down by a cyclically depressed earnings base. On AI capital-spending exposure, it ranks high but with automotive-SiC diversification the pure-plays lack.

The important caveat: the entire cohort has re-rated on the AI-power theme. 'Cheapest in the group' is a relative statement — the group could de-rate together if the durability of AI capital spending is called into question.
role_in_scaleout:
onsemi supplies the 800-volt DC, high-voltage power-conversion silicon — silicon carbide devices, power-discrete components, and the FlexPower and PowerTree power-stage portfolio — that distributes and steps down grid power inside AI server racks. This is a genuinely critical, physics-constrained position in the AI data-center power chain; demand is durable as long as AI capital spending and rack power density continue to rise. onsemi is engaged with all the leading hyperscalers.

That said, onsemi is one of several power-semiconductor suppliers competing for these sockets — Infineon, Power Integrations, Monolithic Power Systems (MPS), and Vicor are all present. The position is critical but not exclusive.
consensus_delta:
Where we disagree with the market cuts in both directions.

The street is no longer looking the wrong way on direction. Consensus 2026 estimated earnings per share has been revised up from $2.90 to $3.09, 2027 estimates sit at $4.36, and the average analyst price target of roughly $171 implies about 30% upside — meaning the bull case is now the consensus view, not a variant one.

Where the market may still underestimate: the structural nature of the 38.5% gross margin at only 77% utilization. The market models a cyclical margin recovery, but the FabRight cost program, product-mix improvements, and factory divestitures make this a structural step-up with significant operating leverage when volume returns. The above-50% long-term gross-margin target is not fully credited in consensus models.

Where the market may overestimate: the AI data-center leg. Analysts extrapolate 'doubling on doubling' off an undisclosed base as if it scales linearly to a material earnings driver, but this segment is still less than 10% of revenue, and management's reluctance to disclose the absolute dollar figure should temper confidence, not inflate it.

The factually fragile consensus assumption is that the first-quarter inflection is a clean V-shaped recovery. First-quarter revenue was still down 1% sequentially, and the recovery is content-driven into a Chinese auto market experiencing a price war where 55% design share can coexist with falling average selling prices.

The bottom line: the easy variant-perception trade — buying the $60 trough — is closed. What remains is a fairly-priced quality compounder, not a mispricing.
valley_of_death:
There is no solvency or cash-burn window here. onsemi generated a record $1.4 billion in full-year 2025 free cash flow at a 24% margin through the worst of the cycle, carries only modest net debt (roughly $2.4 billion in cash and short-term investments against about $3.0 billion in long-term debt), has comfortable interest coverage, and is actively buying back stock. There is no debt maturity wall or burn risk.

The risky window is purely one of valuation and earnings-per-share expectations, not balance-sheet stress. Three specific tripwires over the next three to nine months:

First, the next two quarterly earnings prints — second quarter (early August 2026, guided at $1.535 to $1.635 billion in revenue) and third quarter (early November 2026) — must keep beating an already-elevated bar. A single sequential miss or an AI data-center growth deceleration would pressure a stock that has re-rated to roughly 28 times forward earnings after a 117% run.

Second, Chinese electric-vehicle SiC average-selling-price compression could stall the 38.5%-to-50% gross-margin bridge the current multiple is paying for.

Third, a macro tripwire: if a major hyperscaler guides AI capital spending lower, the entire power-semiconductor group would likely sell off together, since AI-infrastructure spending represents roughly 40% of 2026 S&P 500 earnings-per-share growth and ON now trades as an AI-capex-adjacent name. The risk here is a de-rating, not a default.
positioning_washout:
Forced-selling pressure from mechanical or passive sources is limited. onsemi pays no meaningful dividend, so there is no income-oriented shareholder base to flush, and it is a large-cap S&P 500 and Nasdaq index constituent, meaning passive holders are structurally sticky.

The more relevant forced-selling risk is factor- and momentum-driven. After a 117% gain over 12 months and a 17% gain in just 30 days, ON has become a momentum vehicle and AI-power thematic basket holding. Its shareholder base has rotated from value and contrarian investors who bought the trough to trend-followers and AI-thematic funds whose positioning is now crowded. If AI capital-spending durability is questioned — the single dominant shared factor across the scale-out basket — the de-grossing (funds reducing exposure across correlated positions simultaneously) would be mechanical and correlated across the entire basket. That shareholder-base hand-off from value buyers to momentum buyers is itself a late-stage signal worth noting.
forcing_function:
No single hard date forces a re-rating higher — the upside catalysts are already firing as a gradual trend rather than a discrete event.

The catalysts that force the thesis to be confirmed or broken are the recurring quarterly earnings prints. The second-quarter 2026 report (early August 2026, guided at $1.535 to $1.635 billion in revenue) is the first test of the company's claim that the second half will be stronger than the first half and that gross margin will keep expanding sequentially — and of whether AI data-center revenue maintains its more-than-30% quarterly growth pace. The third-quarter report (early November 2026) is the follow-through test.

The single cleanest upside-forcing event would be management finally disclosing the AI data-center dollar base, or announcing a named hyperscaler power supply agreement with a quantified contract value. That would let the market underwrite the growth option it is currently estimating from management growth rates alone.

The cleanest downside-forcing event is a sequential revenue miss or an AI data-center deceleration on either of those prints.

Given that this is a trend story — not an event-binary — with the biggest re-rating leg already behind it, the right posture is to wait for the next earnings print to clear the bar before adding exposure.
moderator_conclusion:
onsemi is a genuinely strengthening, high-quality franchise. Best-in-cohort free-cash-flow margin (24%, a record achieved through the worst of the cycle); a structural gross-margin step-up (38.5% at only 77% utilization, bridging toward above 50%); a real auto-SiC content lead (roughly 55% of Beijing Auto Show design wins, first year-over-year automotive growth in seven quarters); and a credible AI data-center power business engaged with every major hyperscaler. Management's $346 million buyback at $60.54 against a $122 stock is the clearest insider-confidence signal in the file and earns a credibility rating of 4 out of 5.

But the investment case must respect the regime's discipline: penalize catalysts that have already fired and reward only hard-dated asymmetry with room to run. onsemi's biggest re-rating leg has already fired — up 117% over 12 months. What remains is a soft, trend-continuation catalyst, not a pre-positioned, pre-catalyst entry. The base-case sum-of-the-parts fair value of roughly $124 sits essentially at the $122 current price. The stock's thesis now hinges on the same single shared macro factor — AI capital-spending durability — as the rest of the scale-out basket, which is exactly the book-concentration risk that deserves a penalty, not an increase in position size.

Risk-to-reward has compressed to roughly 1 to 1 on the base case: 0% to 45% upside versus 25% to 36% downside. This is no longer the painful, ignored trough trade it was at $60. It is a good business at a full, defensible, but no longer cheap multiple whose forward return now depends on continuing to beat an elevated bar quarter after quarter.

Verdict: wait for the wash-out. The thesis is real and the quality is high, but wait for a flush — a sequential revenue scare, an AI data-center deceleration, or a cohort-wide AI-spending sell-off — to restore the asymmetry before sizing in. Conviction 3 of 5. Valuation conviction also 3 of 5: stripping away the catalyst overlay, the sum-of-the-parts says fairly valued to modestly rich — a narrow moat and a return on invested capital of 11 to 12% below the wide-moat threshold cap the intrinsic-value case. This is sound quality at fair price, not mispriced cheapness.
interrogator_dossier:
# Interrogator Forensic Dossier — onsemi (ON)
Scale-out Basket D (Power Semiconductors). Investment focus: silicon carbide. Prepared at capital-allocation grade. Sources: Q1 2026 earnings call and release (May 4, 2026), full-year 2025 results, live market data (June 22, 2026).

## 1. Narrative Arc

onsemi spent 2023 through 2025 as a defensive story: a SiC and automotive/industrial power-analog franchise riding out the worst auto-semiconductor inventory correction in a decade. For roughly seven consecutive quarters automotive revenue declined, industrial was weak, and management's entire message was about structural cost actions, factory rationalization under the FabRight program, exiting non-core businesses, and protecting gross margin through the trough. Revenue fell from a roughly $8.3 billion peak in 2023 to roughly $6.0 billion in full-year 2025. Management's consistent refrain was utilization discipline and free-cash-flow defense — they deliberately under-shipped to hold price.

The first quarter of 2026 is the pivot quarter, and management says so explicitly: CEO Hassane El-Khoury calls it a clear inflection point. Three threads turned at once: automotive returned to year-over-year growth (up 5%) for the first time after seven down quarters, led by SiC content gains in China (roughly 55% SiC share of new electric-vehicle models at the April 2026 Beijing Auto Show); AI data-center revenue grew more than 30% in the quarter, nearly double the high-teens management had guided, and is now expected to double year over year in 2026; and gross margin expanded for a third straight quarter to 38.5%, even in a seasonally slow quarter. The story shifted from surviving the trough to a genuine thesis expansion: an inflection in the core auto-SiC business plus a brand-new AI data-center growth leg layered on top.

## 2. Claims Versus Financials

Key checks: Revenue was $1.513 billion, up 5% year over year but down 1% sequentially. AI data-center grew more than 30% in the quarter and is doubling year over year, but it is embedded in the Power Solutions Group segment with no disclosed absolute dollar figure — the implied annual run rate is roughly $300 to $450 million, small relative to $6 billion in total revenue. Non-GAAP gross margin was 38.5%, up sequentially in a down quarter at only 77% utilization — a structural cost win, not a volume tailwind. Full-year 2025 free cash flow was a record $1.4 billion at a 24% margin. Management repurchased $346 million of stock at an average of $60.54 per share (roughly 160% of quarterly free cash flow) against a current price of roughly $122 — the key insider-confidence signal. On a GAAP basis, results were weak: first-quarter GAAP earnings per share were negative $0.08, GAAP operating margin was negative 3.5%, and full-year 2025 GAAP operating margin collapsed from 25.0% to 1.4% on restructuring and impairment charges. Lead times stretched from 23 to 26 weeks. Every operational claim — margin, cost discipline, free cash flow, and buyback — checks out; the AI data-center 'doubling' is the forward bet, and it is still small in absolute dollars today.

## 3. Tone and Confidence Trajectory

Rising — decisively, for the first time in two years. During 2024 and 2025, management's language was defensive: 'disciplined,' 'protect,' 'trough,' 'under-ship.' The first-quarter 2026 call flipped to offensive: 'clear inflection point,' 'accelerating,' 'nearly double our expected growth rate,' 'preferred power solution,' 'all leading hyperscalers,' with specific named design wins (Treo and T1S Ethernet zonal architecture; Geely and NIO 900-volt platforms; more than 30 active FlexPower programs) tethered to hard metrics (77% utilization, 26-week lead times, operating expenses down 7%). This tone shift is credibility-positive.

## 4. Guidance Credibility

Medium-high. First-quarter 2026 results beat the guidance midpoint on both revenue and earnings per share; AI data-center materially over-delivered (more than 30% versus the guided high-teens) — management appears to have guided the hot line conservatively. Second-quarter guidance ($1.535 to $1.635 billion in revenue, earnings per share of $0.65 to $0.77) implies a return to sequential growth. The above-50% long-term gross-margin target depends on volume recovery, product-mix improvement, and factory divestitures that are not fully within the company's control — hold that target skeptically. Near-term guidance reliability: high. Long-term margin target reliability: medium.

## 5. Analyst Pressure Points

Recurring questions from the sell side center on how large and durable the AI data-center business really is — management gives growth rates but withholds the absolute revenue base, which appears deliberate. Also pressed: the volume assumptions behind the above-50% gross-margin bridge; and Chinese auto-SiC pricing dynamics, where 55% design share can coexist with average-selling-price erosion in a brutal competitive environment. At least one published note already describes ON as overbought ahead of a slower fundamental recovery.

## 6. Red Flags and Green Flags

Green flags: the $346 million share buyback at $60.54 versus a current $122 price (bought the bottom); gross margin expanding on falling utilization (structural, not cyclical); the automotive inflection is real (up 5% year over year after seven down quarters, roughly 55% SiC share at the Beijing show); AI data-center optionality is genuine (all major hyperscalers, more than 30 FlexPower programs); record full-year 2025 free cash flow of $1.4 billion at a 24% margin.

Red flags: ongoing GAAP losses driven by heavy restructuring charges; AI data-center revenue base undisclosed; stock up 117% in 12 months and re-rated to roughly 28 to 40 times forward earnings (the cheap entry is gone); Chinese auto-SiC price war intensifying; sequential revenue was still down 1% in the first quarter.

## 7. Hidden Signals

Management's vocabulary has migrated from factory and utilization language ('fab,' 'utilization,' 'trough') to systems and software language ('PowerTree,' 'FlexPower,' 'XPU,' 'hyperscalers,' 'zonal architecture,' 'software-defined vehicle'). This signals a deliberate repositioning from cyclical power-component supplier to a secular AI-and-EV power-systems company. Capital-allocation discipline has also hardened: the explicit commitment to return 100% of free cash flow and the buyback at the bottom both signal confidence in the business's durability. Non-core asset exits are expected to conclude in 2026, marking the end of the pruning phase and the opening of the growth phase.

## 8. Capital-Allocation Verdict

Strengthening. Operationally and in capital discipline, this is a clearly improving franchise: margin expanding through the trough, record free cash flow, value-accretive buybacks at the bottom, a real auto-SiC inflection, and a credible if still-small AI data-center leg.

The single most important consideration for any position-sizing decision: the re-rating has already happened. The stock is up 117% in a year to roughly 28 to 40 times forward earnings at a roughly $48 billion market cap. The trough-value entry is gone, and much of the AI data-center optionality is now in the price. This is a good business at a full multiple whose thesis now requires the AI capital-spending leg to keep compounding.

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat narrow but real; moat trend stable; secular threat manageable.
skeptic_corrections:
A load-bearing mischaracterization runs through the bear case and the dossier: the file repeatedly states that management 'withholds the denominator' and 'refuses to disclose the AI data-center dollar base,' treating that as a confidence-lowering signal. A primary-source check does not support that framing. onsemi disclosed roughly $250 million or more of AI data-center revenue for full-year 2025 (cited at the fourth-quarter and full-year 2025 results by CEO El-Khoury), with the 2026 'doubling' implying roughly $500 million or more. The base is semi-disclosed, not withheld.

This correction cuts in the bear's favor on substance: the actual base of roughly $250 million is even smaller than the file's backward-calculated estimate of $300 to $450 million, making the 'doubling is small in absolute earnings terms' argument more true, not less.

Two additional figure-handling notes: first, the '$1.4 billion free cash flow at a 24% margin' figure is the full-year 2025 number, not a first-quarter figure — first-quarter 2026 free cash flow was $217.2 million. The file references both but does not misstate either. Second, 'slightly net-indebted' is directionally correct; correctly excluding a net-cash premium.

Everything else verifies: first-quarter revenue $1,513.3 million, non-GAAP gross margin 38.5%, GAAP earnings per share negative $0.08, non-GAAP earnings per share $0.64, $346 million in repurchases at an average of $60.54 (roughly 160% of free cash flow), current price roughly $122 with a market cap of $47.66 billion and a forward price-to-earnings ratio of roughly 36.

The wait-for-the-wash-out verdict, the roughly $124 base sum-of-the-parts fair value sitting essentially at the $122 price, and the 'asymmetry spent after a 117% run' conclusion all survive the review and are verified.
skeptic_evidence: SEC Form 8-K Ex-99.1, onsemi Q1 2026 results (filed 2026-05-04): revenue $1,513.3M, non-GAAP GM 38.5%, GAAP diluted EPS $(0.08), non-GAAP EPS $0.64, Q1 FCF $217.2M, $346M share repurchases; AI data center 'more than doubled YoY' / '>30% sequential' (no per-segment $ in the 8-K). sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/0001097864/000114036126018868/ef20072220_ex99-1.htm

onsemi IR / Q4 & FY2025 results + Q1'26 call coverage (2026-02 / 2026-05): management cited ~$250M+ AI-data-center revenue in FY2025, doubling YoY in 2026 (=>~$500M+) — i.e. the dollar base IS disclosed, refuting the 'withheld denominator' premise (StockTitan / mlq.ai / Fool transcript, 2026-05-04).

stockanalysis.com/stocks/on (read 2026-06-22): price $121.62 (2026-06-18 close), market cap $47.66B, forward P/E ~36, trailing P/E ~86 — confirms the file's $122 / $47.6B and the 're-rated, no-longer-cheap' bear premise.
onsemi Q1'26 capital return verified across SEC 8-K + IR: $346M repurchased at avg $60.54 vs ~$122 now (~160% of FCF) — the 'bought-the-bottom' value tell holds as stated.

scale.role: Vertically-integrated SiC + power-analog leader on two seculars (auto-EV + AI-DC power); the inflection is now.
scale.rationale: The power-semi CORE: vertically-integrated SiC + power-analog leader on a double secular (auto SiC + AI-DC power), broadest franchise in the layer and one of only three names at/below fair value (base ~$124 ~at spot) — a true CORE (~78) where the re-rate is only partly fired with recurring forward proof points. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS, and the headline correction actually cuts FOR the bear only on substance (the AI-DC base is ~$250M, even smaller, so the 'doubling' is modest in P&L terms) while refuting the 'withheld denominator' framing, so a modest ~6pt haircut. The least-overpriced SiC name with the broadest franchise; scale in.
scale.demand_durability: High — dual secular (auto SiC + AI-DC power); the auto leg adds cyclicality but also diversifies the AI bolt.
